questions.text: What is the primary purpose of WASMify in the MST ecosystem?

options.text: To execute off-chain computations with ZK proofs, bridging legacy systems to smart contracts without exposing private data
To replace Solidity as the primary smart contract language
To increase block production speed
To store large files on-chain

questions.explanation: WASMify enables trusted off-chain computation with on-chain verification via ZK proofs, allowing enterprises to connect legacy code to blockchain without exposing confidential data.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

WASMify enables trusted off-chain computation with on-chain verification via ZK proofs, allowing enterprises to connect legacy code to blockchain without exposing confidential data.

questions.text: Which languages can be compiled to WASM and used with WASMify?

options.text: C, C++, Rust, Go, and Python (compiled) - any language that targets LLVM or WASM directly
Only Solidity
Only JavaScript
Only Rust

questions.explanation: WebAssembly is a compilation target supported by LLVM, so any language with an LLVM frontend (C, C++, Rust, Zig, etc.) or that compiles to WASM (Go via TinyGo, Python via Pyodide) can be used with WASMify.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

WebAssembly is a compilation target supported by LLVM, so any language with an LLVM frontend (C, C++, Rust, Zig, etc.) or that compiles to WASM (Go via TinyGo, Python via Pyodide) can be used with WASMify.

questions.text: What does the WASMify on-chain verifier check?

options.text: That the ZK proof is valid for the registered bytecode hash and the claimed output
That the private inputs are correct
That the WASM code was written in a memory-safe language
That the transaction sender has sufficient MSTC balance

questions.explanation: The verifier is a cryptographic component that checks the ZK proof against the registered WASM bytecode hash. It verifies that the specific code was executed correctly and produced the claimed output, without seeing the private inputs.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

The verifier is a cryptographic component that checks the ZK proof against the registered WASM bytecode hash. It verifies that the specific code was executed correctly and produced the claimed output, without seeing the private inputs.

questions.text: How does WASMify differ from a traditional oracle like Chainlink?

options.text: WASMify generates a cryptographic proof of correct execution; Chainlink relies on economic incentives and reputation of node operators
WASMify is centralised, Chainlink is decentralised
WASMify cannot process off-chain data
WASMify requires a token, Chainlink does not

questions.explanation: Chainlink decentralises trust across multiple nodes, but you still trust those nodes to report honestly. WASMify replaces trust entirely with a ZK proof - the correctness is mathematically guaranteed, not economically incentivised.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Chainlink decentralises trust across multiple nodes, but you still trust those nodes to report honestly. WASMify replaces trust entirely with a ZK proof - the correctness is mathematically guaranteed, not economically incentivised.

questions.text: What is registered on-chain before a WASMify module can be used?

options.text: The hash of the WASM bytecode
The entire WASM bytecode
The private input data
The source code of the module

questions.explanation: Only the hash of the WASM bytecode is stored on-chain in the WASMify Registry. The verifier checks that proofs reference a registered hash, ensuring only approved code can generate valid proofs. The full bytecode remains off-chain.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

Only the hash of the WASM bytecode is stored on-chain in the WASMify Registry. The verifier checks that proofs reference a registered hash, ensuring only approved code can generate valid proofs. The full bytecode remains off-chain.

questions.text: Why must WASM code used in WASMify be deterministic?

options.text: Because the ZK prover requires that the same inputs always produce the same outputs; non-deterministic code would generate inconsistent execution traces that cannot be proven
Because MST validators cannot execute random code
Because the EVM is deterministic
Because Solidity does not support randomness

questions.explanation: ZK proof generation relies on a consistent execution trace. If the WASM code behaves non-deterministically (e.g., generates random numbers or depends on system time), different runs produce different traces, and the prover cannot create a valid proof. Determinism is essential for the proof to be reproducible.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A

ZK proof generation relies on a consistent execution trace. If the WASM code behaves non-deterministically (e.g., generates random numbers or depends on system time), different runs produce different traces, and the prover cannot create a valid proof. Determinism is essential for the proof to be reproducible.

questions.text: A pharmaceutical company registers a WASM module (hash 0x9F7A...) that verifies cold-chain compliance. The module takes a temperature log as private input and outputs VALID if all readings are between 2°C and 8°C, otherwise INVALID. A shipment arrives, and the WASMify runtime executes the module with the actual temperature log (which shows one reading of 9.1°C). The ZK prover generates a proof. What will the output be, and what information will be visible on-chain after the proof is verified? Explain why the temperature log itself is not exposed.
questions.explanation:
Output: INVALID (because 9.1°C exceeds the 8°C threshold).
                Visible on-chain: The WASM bytecode hash (0x9F7A...), the output (INVALID), and the ZK proof. The smart contract can verify the proof and record that the shipment failed compliance.
                Why the temperature log is not exposed: The temperature log is a private input to the WASM module. The ZK proof cryptographically attests that the module was executed correctly on some inputs that produced the output INVALID, without revealing what those inputs were. The on-chain verifier checks the proof without knowing the inputs - a core property of zero-knowledge proofs.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Expected Answer & Marks Breakdown (3 marks)

Output: INVALID (because 9.1°C exceeds the 8°C threshold).

Visible on-chain: The WASM bytecode hash (0x9F7A...), the output (INVALID), and the ZK proof. The smart contract can verify the proof and record that the shipment failed compliance.

Why the temperature log is not exposed: The temperature log is a private input to the WASM module. The ZK proof cryptographically attests that the module was executed correctly on some inputs that produced the output INVALID, without revealing what those inputs were. The on-chain verifier checks the proof without knowing the inputs - a core property of zero-knowledge proofs.

Marks breakdown (3 marks):
• 1 mark: Correctly identifies the output as INVALID.
• 1 mark: States that the bytecode hash, output, and proof are visible on-chain.
• 1 mark: Explains that the temperature log is a private input and the ZK proof hides it while still proving correct execution.

questions.text: "WASMify requires the enterprise to upload its private business data to an MST validator node so that the ZK proof can be generated on-chain."
questions.explanation:
1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
                +1 mark: Explains that the WASM runtime and ZK prover run off-chain inside the enterprise, and only the proof (not private data) is sent to the blockchain.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: Correctly states FALSE.
+1 mark: Explains that the WASM runtime and ZK prover run off-chain inside the enterprise, and only the proof (not private data) is sent to the blockchain.

questions.text:
Design a WASMify-based solution for a parametric crop insurance product in Maharashtra. Farmers pay a premium, and if the total rainfall during the monsoon season (June - September) falls below 500 mm as measured by a network of government-certified IoT rain gauges, an automatic payout is triggered.

Your answer must include:
(a) What data is private and what data is public.
(b) What the WASM module computes and in what language it could be written.
(c) The full flow from sensor data collection to on-chain payout, referencing the WASMify components (runtime, ZK prover, on-chain verifier, registry).
(d) Why a traditional oracle (like Chainlink) would be insufficient for this use case.
(e) What the farmer, the insurer, and the regulator each see on-chain after a payout event.
questions.explanation:
Model Answer:
(a) Private vs Public Data: Private: Individual rain gauge readings (lat/long, mm per hour), the exact rainfall calculation algorithm, the insurer's proprietary risk model parameters. Public: The WASM bytecode hash, the final aggregated rainfall index (e.g., "total monsoon rainfall at district X = 412 mm"), the payout decision (PAID/NOT PAID), and the ZK proof.
(b) WASM Module: The module computes the total monsoon rainfall from the raw sensor data, compares it against the 500 mm threshold, and outputs either PAYOUT or NO_PAYOUT along with the computed index. It could be written in Rust (for memory safety and performance) or Python (if the existing actuarial model is in Python, compiled via Pyodide to WASM).
(c) Full Flow:
                1. Registration: The insurer compiles the rainfall calculation module to WASM and registers its bytecode hash in the WASMify Registry on MST.
                2. Data Collection: Government rain gauges push encrypted readings to the insurer's secure server throughout the monsoon.
                3. Execution: After September 30, the WASMify Runtime (running on the insurer's infrastructure) executes the WASM module with all accumulated sensor data as private input.
                4. Proving: The ZK Prover generates a proof that the registered WASM bytecode was executed correctly, producing the output (e.g., "RAINFALL = 412 mm, PAYOUT = TRUE") from valid sensor inputs, without revealing the raw data.
                5. Verification: The proof and output are submitted to the InsurancePayout smart contract on MST. The contract calls the WASMify Verifier precompile, which checks the proof against the registered bytecode hash.
                6. Payout: If the proof is valid and the output is PAYOUT, the smart contract automatically transfers MSTC/stablecoin to the farmer's wallet.
(d) Why Traditional Oracle Is Insufficient: A traditional oracle would require the insurer to expose raw rain gauge data to the oracle nodes, violating data confidentiality agreements with the government. The oracle nodes would need to be trusted to execute the rainfall calculation correctly. In WASMify, the ZK proof guarantees correct execution without trusting any third party - the cryptography replaces the trust.
(e) What Each Party Sees On-Chain:
                • Farmer: Sees that the PAYOUT transaction was executed for their policy, with the rainfall index (412 mm) and a cryptographic proof that the calculation was fair.
                • Insurer: Sees the same on-chain data plus the internal records; can cryptographically prove to auditors that payouts were only made when justified.
                • Regulator (e.g., IRDAI): Can audit the entire process - verifies that the WASM bytecode hash registered on-chain matches the approved rainfall calculation methodology, and that all payouts were triggered by valid proofs. The regulator does not need access to raw sensor data to verify compliance.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Model Answer & Marks Breakdown (9 marks)

Model Answer:

(a) Private vs Public Data: Private: Individual rain gauge readings (lat/long, mm per hour), the exact rainfall calculation algorithm, the insurer's proprietary risk model parameters. Public: The WASM bytecode hash, the final aggregated rainfall index (e.g., "total monsoon rainfall at district X = 412 mm"), the payout decision (PAID/NOT PAID), and the ZK proof.

(b) WASM Module: The module computes the total monsoon rainfall from the raw sensor data, compares it against the 500 mm threshold, and outputs either PAYOUT or NO_PAYOUT along with the computed index. It could be written in Rust (for memory safety and performance) or Python (if the existing actuarial model is in Python, compiled via Pyodide to WASM).

(c) Full Flow:
1. Registration: The insurer compiles the rainfall calculation module to WASM and registers its bytecode hash in the WASMify Registry on MST.
2. Data Collection: Government rain gauges push encrypted readings to the insurer's secure server throughout the monsoon.
3. Execution: After September 30, the WASMify Runtime (running on the insurer's infrastructure) executes the WASM module with all accumulated sensor data as private input.
4. Proving: The ZK Prover generates a proof that the registered WASM bytecode was executed correctly, producing the output (e.g., "RAINFALL = 412 mm, PAYOUT = TRUE") from valid sensor inputs, without revealing the raw data.
5. Verification: The proof and output are submitted to the InsurancePayout smart contract on MST. The contract calls the WASMify Verifier precompile, which checks the proof against the registered bytecode hash.
6. Payout: If the proof is valid and the output is PAYOUT, the smart contract automatically transfers MSTC/stablecoin to the farmer's wallet.

(d) Why Traditional Oracle Is Insufficient: A traditional oracle would require the insurer to expose raw rain gauge data to the oracle nodes, violating data confidentiality agreements with the government. The oracle nodes would need to be trusted to execute the rainfall calculation correctly. In WASMify, the ZK proof guarantees correct execution without trusting any third party - the cryptography replaces the trust.

(e) What Each Party Sees On-Chain:
• Farmer: Sees that the PAYOUT transaction was executed for their policy, with the rainfall index (412 mm) and a cryptographic proof that the calculation was fair.
• Insurer: Sees the same on-chain data plus the internal records; can cryptographically prove to auditors that payouts were only made when justified.
• Regulator (e.g., IRDAI): Can audit the entire process - verifies that the WASM bytecode hash registered on-chain matches the approved rainfall calculation methodology, and that all payouts were triggered by valid proofs. The regulator does not need access to raw sensor data to verify compliance.

Marks breakdown (9 marks):
• 2 marks: (a) Correctly identifies private vs public data, demonstrating understanding of confidentiality boundaries.
• 1 mark: (b) Describes the WASM module's computation and language choice.
• 3 marks: (c) Fully traces the flow through all four WASMify components (registration, runtime, prover, verifier) to on-chain payout.
• 1 mark: (d) Explains why a traditional oracle is insufficient (data exposure, trust assumptions).
• 2 marks: (e) Describes what each stakeholder sees on-chain and why it satisfies their requirements.
